retrieved_contexts: Title: Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Semantic and Structural Integration for Large Language Model Reasoning | Authors: Hyewon Lee, Sungsu Lim | Categories: nan | Published: 2026-02-26 | Abstract: Recent GraphRAG methods based on knowledge graphs (KGs) primarily rely on either under-reasoning or a structural path-level retriever, which prevents them from jointly capturing fine-grained semantic relevance and explicit multi-hop reasoning paths. This separation often results in semantic mismatch—where logical links are missing—or structural over-constraint in reasoning— where rigid dependencies limit flexible reasoning—thereby degrading both answer accuracy and the reliability of evidence in complex KGQA tasks. To address these issues, we propose HybRAG, a hybrid retrieval framework that synergistically integrates a semantic node-level retriever and structural path-level retriever. HybRAG constructs a hybrid subgraph that jointly reflects the semantic proximity of entities and the relational structures encoded in the KG. Furthermore, we incorporate retrieval-augmented fine-tuning, which enables the model to internalize advanced reasoning strategies for interpreting disparate semantic and structural signals, rather than merely memorizing domain facts. Through extensive experiments on the WebQSP and CWQ benchmarks, we demonstrate that HybRAG effectively bridges the gap between LLM-centric semantic approaches and GNN-centric structural approaches, outperforming single-retriever baselines. Our findings, including detailed sensitivity and ablation analyses, provide empirical evidence that the systematic alignment of semantic and structural signals is essential for ensuring the reasoning reliability and scalability of next-generation GraphRAG systems. | Journal: Applied Sciences

answer: This study presents a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) based system designed to provide farmers with expert agricultural advisory services.
retrieved_contexts: Title: Empowering farmers with artificial intelligence: a retrieval-augmented generation based large language model advisory framework | Authors: Shreeram Sawant, Rahul Nair, Siddharth Hariharan | Categories: nan | Published: 2026-01-27 | Abstract: This study presents a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) based system designed to provide farmers with expert agricultural advisory services. The framework delivers context aware guidance on critical practices such as crop cultivation, pest and disease management, fertilizer application, and other agronomic practices, and compares the performance of four large language models (LLMs) in generating these recommendations. The system processes package of practices (PoP) documents for five major crops maize, ragi, sweet potato, cotton, and groundnut through semantic chunking and embedding using Amazon Titan via BedrockEmbeddings. Vector representations are indexed in ChromaDB to enable efficient similarity search for query-relevant content retrieval. Upon receiving user queries, the system retrieves the most semantically similar document chunks and incorporates them into structured prompts. Four LLMs such as Llama3.1, Mistral, Phi3, and Qwen2.5 were evaluated for their effectiveness in generating accurate agricultural recommendations. Performance was evaluated across multiple dimensions. Relevance and retrieval were assessed using precision@K, recall@K, mean reciprocal rank (MRR), and normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG). Lexical overlap was measured with the bilingual evaluation understudy (BLEU) and recall-oriented understudy for gisting evaluation (ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L) metrics. Semantic quality was analyzed using Bidirectional Encoder Representations from transformers score (BERTScore) precision, recall, F1, semantic similarity and faithfulness to capture contextual alignment between generated and reference responses. Source attribution was assessed through the attribution score, while efficiency was measured using retrieval time, generation time, and total time. Overall, mistral and Qwen2.5 achieved the highest performance, demonstrating superior relevance, semantic quality, and efficiency. This evaluation highlights which LLMs perform best for the agricultural domain and illustrates the potential of knowledge-grounded AI systems to democratize agricultural expertise, particularly in regions with limited access to traditional advisory services. | Journal: Journal of Agricultural Engineering

A retrieval augmented generation approach is employed to gather the relevant agricultural information prior to generating advisory responses, thus preventing any hallucinations or out-of-date recommendations. answer: <div> This survey provides a comprehensive analysis of Agentic GraphRAG, an emerging paradigm that integrates Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Agentic RAG.&nbsp;<span>While large language models know a lot, their knowledge is frozen at training time and they often suffer from hallucinations.
retrieved_contexts: Title: A Survey of Agentic GraphRAG: From Retrieval-augmented Generation to Graph-native Agents | Authors: Zihan Chen, Lei Zheng, Di Zhu | Categories: nan | Published: 2026-01-01 | Abstract: <div> This survey provides a comprehensive analysis of Agentic GraphRAG, an emerging paradigm that integrates Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with Agentic RAG.&nbsp;<span>While large language models know a lot, their knowledge is frozen at training time and they often suffer from hallucinations. RAG is the standard workaround: before the model answers, fetch relevant text from an external corpus and generate response based on the it. Two lines of work have pushed RAG in different directions: (1) GraphRAG changes what the model retrieves from: instead of a pile of text chunks indexed by similarity, it builds a knowledge graph of entities and their relations, summarizes densely connected regions, and retrieves over that. (2) Agentic RAG changes how retrieval happens: moving beyond one shot generation based on user query, an agent plans, acts, critiques its own work, keeps memory across steps, and then retrieves multiple times as needed. Recent work combines the two, with Karpathy's LLM Wiki as a salient example of non-tech-savvy solutions. The agent's planning, acting, critiquing, and remembering all operate over graph operations. The graph stops being a passive lookup table and becomes the space the agent moves through. This survey treats such combination as a research area in its own right. We organize recent primary papers on a 4x3 grid (four agent roles vs. three graph operations) grounded in a central observation: pure GraphRAG and standalone Agentic RAG each possess recurring failure modes, and the combination of the two paradigms can effectively mitigate these. The body of the survey covers seven method families that populate the grid: single-agent iterative traversal, self-evolving GraphRAG, RL-trained graph walkers, multi-agent graph systems, agentic graph construction, persistent graph memory, and hybrid structured–unstructured retrieval. Three application areas ground the discussion: medicine, legal reasoning, and cyber threat intelligence. Furthermore, recent papers reach directly opposing conclusions about whether graphs are still needed once an agent is in the loop. We trace where the disagreement comes from, and discuss evaluation protocols that jointly score agent trajectories and retrievals, alongside the adversarial robustness of agent–graph systems.</span> </div> | Journal: nan

Title: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems for Medical Decision Support: A Comprehensive Survey | Authors: Venkitesh S. Anand, Akhil V. V. | Categories: nan | Published: 2026-01-01 | Abstract: The introduction of Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation Technology is a breakthrough in the field of Medical Artificial Intelligence, allowing autonomous agents to coordinate complex clinical workflows, integrate evidence into the patient’s care cycle, and supply decision-making support to health care professionals and patients. In this manuscript, we will offer a comprehensive analysis of the architectures of Agentic RAG technology and its numerous applications and approaches within healthcare. More specifically, we will systematically evaluate the various types of multi-agentic systems that may be employed for specific professional and clinical tasks, such as diagnostic reasoning, medical question answering, report generation, drug discovery, and optimizing overall healthcare delivery. Aspects of the technical architecture that will be addressed in this review are the integration of knowledge graphs to represent knowledge, multi-modal reasoning capabilities, mechanisms to support adherence to clinical guidelines, and creation of interpretable decision-making frameworks. In addition to these key technological developments, the author will also address a variety of challenges encountered when developing agentic RAG systems, including clinical validation, collaboration between health care providers and AI, ethical issues, and deploying agentic RAG technology in health care settings that have limited resources. Lastly, this review provides an overarching conceptual framework for understanding how agentic RAG technology can assist in supporting physicians, nursing staff, health care personnel, and patients with a variety of tasks across multiple disciplines, while also supporting many of the key safety, privacy, and responsible considerations related to AI in health care. This review identifies architecturally based patterns in medical agentic systems. | Journal: nan

Title: Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Semantic and Structural Integration for Large Language Model Reasoning | Authors: Hyewon Lee, Sungsu Lim | Categories: nan | Published: 2026-02-26 | Abstract: Recent GraphRAG methods based on knowledge graphs (KGs) primarily rely on either under-reasoning or a structural path-level retriever, which prevents them from jointly capturing fine-grained semantic relevance and explicit multi-hop reasoning paths. This separation often results in semantic mismatch—where logical links are missing—or structural over-constraint in reasoning— where rigid dependencies limit flexible reasoning—thereby degrading both answer accuracy and the reliability of evidence in complex KGQA tasks. To address these issues, we propose HybRAG, a hybrid retrieval framework that synergistically integrates a semantic node-level retriever and structural path-level retriever. HybRAG constructs a hybrid subgraph that jointly reflects the semantic proximity of entities and the relational structures encoded in the KG. Furthermore, we incorporate retrieval-augmented fine-tuning, which enables the model to internalize advanced reasoning strategies for interpreting disparate semantic and structural signals, rather than merely memorizing domain facts. Through extensive experiments on the WebQSP and CWQ benchmarks, we demonstrate that HybRAG effectively bridges the gap between LLM-centric semantic approaches and GNN-centric structural approaches, outperforming single-retriever baselines. Our findings, including detailed sensitivity and ablation analyses, provide empirical evidence that the systematic alignment of semantic and structural signals is essential for ensuring the reasoning reliability and scalability of next-generation GraphRAG systems. | Journal: Applied Sciences
